qa_pairs.question: When should a Principal Investigator (PI) accept a Fast Track invitation for an RRG application?
qa_pairs.answer: PIs should accept a Fast Track invitation if their needs for resources will remain the same, knowing that all allocations are still subject to scaling based on supply and demand and on the amount of resources requested in their previous RRG full application.

qa_pairs.question: When should a Principal Investigator (PI) submit a new RRG application instead of using Fast Track?
qa_pairs.answer: If a PI needs more resources than what they currently have allocated, especially for compute or cloud resources, they should submit a new RRG application.

qa_pairs.question: Are all applications submitted to the Resource Allocation Competition (RAC) accepted?
qa_pairs.answer: No. Due to the imbalance of demand versus available resources, the RAC has increasingly become a more competitive process, meaning not all applications are accepted.

qa_pairs.question: Where can applicants find the evaluation criteria for RAC applications?
qa_pairs.answer: The evaluation criteria for both the Resources for Research Groups (RRG) and Research Platforms and Portals (RPP) application processes are available in the RAC application guide.

qa_pairs.question: What is the primary purpose of the Research Platforms and Portals (RPP) application process?
qa_pairs.answer: The RPP is a peer-reviewed application process for projects whose primary purpose is to provide a service through scientific gateways that improve access to shared datasets, enhance existing online research tools and facilities, or advance national or international research collaborations.

qa_pairs.question: What is the primary purpose of the Resources for Research Groups (RRG) application process?
qa_pairs.answer: The RRG is a peer-reviewed application process for projects whose primary purpose is to conduct research requiring compute, storage and cloud resources to meet their goals.

qa_pairs.question: Which application process should be used for projects primarily needing compute resources in a cluster to conduct research?
qa_pairs.answer: Projects primarily needing compute resources in a cluster to conduct research should apply through the RRG process.

qa_pairs.question: What are the main requirements for projects applying through the RPP process?
qa_pairs.answer: Projects applying through the RPP must provide resources to a larger research community via cloud-based tools, applications, and/or data, and be able to develop, operate, and manage the proposed portal or platform with minimal support from Alliance Federation staff.

qa_pairs.question: What types of resources are typically involved in an RPP project?
qa_pairs.answer: Generally, an RPP project will involve cloud resources, usually through the development of a front-end gateway on persistent virtual machines, with possible backend compute either through cloud compute nodes or job-based submission to large national clusters. Many also include large databases.

qa_pairs.question: What level of experience is expected from teams applying for RPPs?
qa_pairs.answer: Applicants for RPPs are expected to have sufficient experience within their team to develop, manage, and operate their platform or portal.

qa_pairs.question: Can an Academic Principal Investigator (PI) be the lead submitter for more than one RPP application?
qa_pairs.answer: Yes, an Academic PI can be the lead submitter for one RPP application per competition round and can also be involved in other RPP applications as a participant.

qa_pairs.question: Can an Academic Principal Investigator (PI) be the lead submitter for more than one RRG application?
qa_pairs.answer: An Academic PI can apply as a lead submitter for only one RRG application (either through the full application process or the Fast Track one) at a time, but can be involved in multiple RRG submissions as a participant.

qa_pairs.question: Do research groups with multi-year RPP allocations need to submit a full application every year for renewal?
qa_pairs.answer: No, research groups that are awarded multi-year allocations are required to submit an annual progress report, but they do not need to submit a new application each year to renew.

qa_pairs.question: If I have a one-year RPP allocation and want to keep it for the next year, do I have to submit a new application?
qa_pairs.answer: Yes, if you have a one-year RPP allocation and want to keep it for the next year, you must submit a new application.

qa_pairs.question: Where can I find the evaluation criteria for RPP applications?
qa_pairs.answer: The evaluation criteria for RPP applications are available in the application guide.

qa_pairs.question: Where can I find information about what is required for the RPP annual progress report?
qa_pairs.answer: Details about the information required for the RPP annual progress report can be found on the RPP Annual Progress Report page.

qa_pairs.question: What is the meaning of the term 'allocation' in the context of advanced research computing?
qa_pairs.answer: A compute allocation corresponds to a target for use over a period of time, usually a year. For storage and certain cloud resources, an allocation signifies a maximum amount. Allocations are typically made in terms of core years, GPU years, or storage space.

qa_pairs.question: How are storage allocations understood by research groups?
qa_pairs.answer: Storage allocations are the most straightforward to understand: research groups will get a maximum amount of storage that they can use exclusively throughout the allocation period.

qa_pairs.question: What units are typically used for allocations?
qa_pairs.answer: Allocations are usually made in terms of core years, GPU years, or storage space.

qa_pairs.question: When are successful allocations generally activated?
qa_pairs.answer: Generally, all successful allocations are activated in April. The exact starting date will be confirmed in your notification letter.
